scenario_name: Vishing attempt — employee refuses and verifies via official channel
chunking_insight: SB080 refusal spans C1-C2; both approaches should detect reliably but chunked must aggregate C1+C2 to confirm full refusal-then-verify pattern. SB013 is clear in C3 alone.
benchmark_notes: Textbook SB080. Employee refuses unsolicited credential request, independently verifies via official IT number, and commits to formal incident report. Low FP/FN risk.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Caller: Hello, this is James from the IT department. I'm calling because we've detected some unusual activity on your account and need to verify your credentials immediately. || [00:10] Employee: Okay, what kind of unusual activity? || [00:14] Caller: Multiple failed login attempts from overseas. To secure your account, I need your current username and password. || [00:22] Employee: That's strange. I wasn't expecting this call. Are you actually from IT? Our security training says IT never asks for passwords over the phone. || [00:31] Caller: I understand your concern, but this is urgent — we need to act quickly before your account is fully compromised. || [00:38] Employee: I'm not comfortable sharing my password like this. I'm going to hang up and call the IT helpdesk directly using the number on the company intranet. || [00:47] Caller: That will take too long — || [00:49] Employee: I am sorry, I need to follow proper procedure. [employee hangs up] || [00:54] [Employee dials official IT helpdesk number] || [01:02] IT Support: IT Helpdesk, how can I help? || [01:04] Employee: Hi, I just received a call from someone claiming to be from IT asking for my password. I did not give anything out. Is there actually an issue with my account? || [01:14] IT Support: Good call — we never ask for passwords by phone. There is no alert on your account right now. That was almost certainly a social engineering attempt. || [01:24] Employee: Understood. I will submit this via the security incident portal as well. || [01:27] IT Support: Please do. Flag it as a suspected vishing call and we will investigate the number.

scenario_name: Phishing email reported with full indicators check
chunking_insight: All four behaviors are distributed across distinct chunks; chunked and full-context should perform similarly. SB081 appears early, SB020 in C1, SB082+SB013 in C2. No misleading signals.
benchmark_notes: Employee demonstrates SB081 (identifies urgency, threat language, spoofed domain), SB020 (hovered over link before clicking), SB082 (calls IT to verify), and SB013 (logs incident). Strong multi-behavior TP.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Employee: Hi, this is Priya from Finance. I just received a really strange email and wanted to check with you before doing anything with it. || [00:08] IT Support: Of course, go ahead. || [00:11] Employee: It claims to be from our bank. It says: Urgent — your corporate account has been suspended due to suspicious transactions. Click here to restore access within 24 hours or your account will be permanently closed. || [00:24] IT Support: Good instinct to call us straight away. That urgency and the threat of permanent closure are classic phishing tactics. || [00:31] Employee: I noticed that too. The sender address was something like support@corporatebanking-secure.net — definitely not our actual bank's domain. || [00:41] IT Support: That is a clear red flag. Did you hover over the link to check where it actually goes? || [00:46] Employee: Yes — I hovered over it and the URL that appeared was a completely different address with lots of numbers and an unusual domain. I did not click it. || [00:56] IT Support: You did exactly the right thing. Please do not click anything in that email. Can you forward it to phishing@company.com so we can analyse it? || [01:06] Employee: Already done. Should I also log it in the security portal? || [01:10] IT Support: Yes, please submit a formal incident report. You caught this early — it may be targeting others in Finance as well. || [01:19] Employee: Will do. Is there anything else to watch out for? || [01:23] IT Support: Keep an eye out for follow-up calls or messages claiming to be from IT or the bank. Always verify unexpected communications using known official contact details before responding.

scenario_name: Security coaching for new employee — passwords and link safety
chunking_insight: Behaviors are well-distributed one-per-chunk and each chunk independently signals the relevant behavior. Chunked approach should match full-context performance.
benchmark_notes: Multi-behavior coaching conversation. SB014 (seeking help), SB016 (unique passwords), SB003 (strong password), SB020 (link checking). Clean TP with no misleading content. Good baseline for multi-behavior recall.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] New Employee: Hi, I just started last week in the marketing team and have a few questions about the security rules I need to follow. || [00:09] Security Coach: Of course, happy to help. What is on your mind? || [00:12] New Employee: I have been using the same password for all my accounts for years. Is that a problem here? || [00:18] Security Coach: That is actually one of the most important things to change. Using the same password everywhere is risky — if one service gets breached, attackers can try those credentials on all your other accounts. Every account needs its own unique password. || [00:30] New Employee: Understood. What makes a password strong? || [00:33] Security Coach: At least 12 characters, avoid personal information like names or birthdays. A good technique is three random words strung together — something like BlueTruckLemon. Even better, use the company-approved password manager and I will show you how to set that up. || [00:47] New Employee: What about clicking links in emails? I get a lot of them. || [00:52] Security Coach: Before clicking any link, hover your cursor over it to see the actual destination URL that appears at the bottom of your browser. If it looks suspicious, does not match the sender's domain, or has unusual characters, do not click. || [01:05] New Employee: What if I accidentally click something that turns out to be bad? || [01:09] Security Coach: Report it to IT immediately via the security portal or just call us directly. Early reporting limits the damage. Do not wait out of embarrassment — it happens to everyone and quick action is what matters. || [01:20] New Employee: Thanks, that is really helpful. I feel much better prepared now.

scenario_name: Unsolicited MFA code triggers password change and incident report
chunking_insight: Behaviors span across chunks but each chunk contains a clear positive signal. SB001 is demonstrated in C0 (employee questions unsolicited MFA code) and C1 (does not share the code). SB013 peaks in C2.
benchmark_notes: Employee demonstrates SB001 (received unsolicited MFA code and did not share it), SB003 (told to create strong new password), SB013 (logs incident in portal). IT confirms MFA blocked a real credential attack. Clear TP.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Employee: Hi IT, I am a bit worried. I just got a text with a login authentication code for my work account but I was not trying to sign in. || [00:11] IT Support: Thanks for calling right away — that is important to flag immediately. Someone may have your password and is attempting to sign in. Your MFA code is the only thing blocking them right now. || [00:21] Employee: I have not clicked anything or shared the code with anyone. || [00:25] IT Support: Good. Never share that code with anyone, even someone claiming to be from IT. What I need you to do now is log in yourself and change your password immediately. || [00:35] Employee: Should it be the same style of password as before? || [00:38] IT Support: Make it completely different — a strong passphrase, at least 12 characters, no personal information, and not used on any other account whatsoever. || [00:46] Employee: Changing it right now. Should I log this somewhere as well? || [00:50] IT Support: Yes — please file a security incident report in the portal. Include the time you received the code, which account it was for, and any other details you remember. || [00:59] Employee: Done. Can you check the account from your side? || [01:02] IT Support: I am on it — I can see a failed login attempt from an IP address in Eastern Europe about eight minutes ago. MFA blocked it successfully. I will flag your account for monitoring and escalate this to the security team. || [01:14] Employee: Should I be worried about my personal accounts as well? || [01:17] IT Support: Check whether you use the same password on any personal accounts and change those too. Credential stuffing is common — attackers try stolen passwords across many services automatically.

scenario_name: Remote worker device security — locking, shutdown, and wireless hygiene
chunking_insight: Each behavior maps almost perfectly to one 30-second chunk. Chunked detection should perform as well as or better than full-context here. Ideal test case for per-chunk behavior isolation.
benchmark_notes: Four distinct device security behaviors each demonstrated in a separate time window. Ideal for validating that chunk-level detection can correctly attribute behaviors to individual windows without cross-contamination. NOTE: employee had pre-existing violations (SB038, SB039) that the coaching call corrects.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Security Manager: Hi, this is a quick follow-up call from the security team about your remote working practices. We noticed from the access logs that you have been working from a cafe recently. Do you have a few minutes? || [00:11] Employee: Of course — I try to be careful but I am happy to hear your guidance. || [00:15] Security Manager: Good. When you step away from your laptop, even briefly to get a coffee, do you lock your screen? || [00:22] Employee: Yes, I always lock it — I use Windows key and L every time I step away. || [00:27] Security Manager: Perfect. What about end of day — do you leave it on standby or fully shut it down? || [00:33] Employee: I usually just close the lid. Is that not okay? || [00:36] Security Manager: We recommend a full shutdown, especially for remote work. It takes your machine offline, ensures any pending security updates are applied, and reduces overnight exposure. Sleep mode keeps the system partially active. || [00:48] Employee: I will start doing that from tonight. What about Bluetooth — I leave it on all the time. || [00:53] Security Manager: Disable it when you are not actively using a Bluetooth device. In public spaces, Bluetooth can be exploited by nearby attackers. Same applies to AirDrop on Mac. || [01:01] Employee: I will turn it off right now actually. || [01:03] Security Manager: Good. And are you using the company VPN whenever you are on cafe or public Wi-Fi? || [01:08] Employee: Yes — I always connect to the VPN before I start any work. || [01:12] Security Manager: Excellent. You are doing most things right — just add the full shutdown habit and the wireless discipline and you will be in great shape.

scenario_name: Legitimate bank customer service call — PIN verification in one chunk
misleading_chunk_explanation: The chunk between 00:25 and 00:36 contains the bank agent asking the customer to confirm their online banking password or 4-digit PIN. In isolation this sounds identical to a vishing attempt (violating SB080). Full context shows this is a standard identity verification step on a legitimate inbound customer service call initiated by the customer — the opposite of a vishing scenario.
chunking_insight: Full-context model correctly identifies this as legitimate customer service. Chunked model may fire SB080 as a false positive on C1 alone. Key test for specificity of chunk-based detection.
benchmark_notes: Critical FP test case. The phrase confirm your online banking password or security PIN in chunk C1 is syntactically identical to vishing attack language. Only full-context reveals (a) the customer called the bank proactively, (b) this is standard verification procedure. A chunked model with no context window will likely FP here.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Bank Agent: Thank you for calling Meridian Bank customer service. My name is Sarah. How can I help you today? || [00:07] Customer: Hi, I am calling about a transaction on my account that I do not recognise. || [00:12] Bank Agent: I can help with that. I will need to verify your identity first. Can you please confirm the full name on the account? || [00:18] Customer: It is Michael Thompson. || [00:20] Bank Agent: Thank you. And can you confirm your date of birth? || [00:23] Customer: The 14th of March 1985. || [00:26] Bank Agent: Great. For additional security verification, could you please confirm your online banking password or the 4-digit security PIN associated with this account? || [00:34] Customer: Sure, my PIN is 4721. || [00:37] Bank Agent: Thank you, Mr Thompson. I can now access your account. I can see a transaction for 249 pounds at an electronics retailer on the 28th — did you authorise that purchase? || [00:48] Customer: No I did not. I have never heard of that store. || [00:52] Bank Agent: In that case I will raise a dispute and block further transactions on this card immediately. A replacement card will arrive within three to five working days. || [01:02] Customer: That is perfect, thank you. Is there anything I need to do on my end? || [01:05] Bank Agent: Nothing at all — we will handle everything. You will receive a written confirmation by post. Is there anything else I can help you with today? || [01:12] Customer: No, that is everything. Thank you so much. || [01:14] Bank Agent: You are welcome. Have a great day, Mr Thompson.

scenario_name: Marketing team reviewing campaign copy that resembles phishing language
misleading_chunk_explanation: The chunk between 00:42 and 01:14 contains a marketing designer quoting proposed email copy that includes phrases like verify your details now, act immediately or lose access, your account will be suspended, and confirm email and password. In isolation this chunk is nearly indistinguishable from an employee describing a phishing email (SB081 pattern). Full context makes clear this is a marketing team critiquing their own campaign draft to avoid it looking like phishing — the opposite of a security incident.
chunking_insight: Chunks C2 and C3 contain explicit phishing-style language. Full-context model can use C0-C1 setup (marketing meeting framing) to disambiguate. Chunked model with no preceding context will very likely FP on SB081 in C2-C3.
benchmark_notes: High-difficulty FP. Tests whether a model can distinguish between describing phishing language (SB081 TP) versus using phishing-style language in a non-security context. The misleading content spans almost two full chunk windows. Strong test for both precision and contextual grounding.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Marketing Manager: Right, let us go through the email campaign draft before it goes out this afternoon. || [00:06] Email Designer: Sure. The subject line is: Your exclusive offer expires in 24 hours — do not miss out. || [00:13] Marketing Manager: Good urgency in the subject line. What is the main call to action in the body? || [00:17] Email Designer: There is a prominent red button that says Click here to claim your discount now. The link goes through to our promotional landing page. || [00:25] Marketing Manager: Does the link destination look clean to the customer? I want them to trust it. || [00:30] Email Designer: It redirects through our tracking system first so the URL in the email is quite long with lots of parameters. || [00:37] Marketing Manager: We should clean that up — security-aware customers check where links go before clicking and a messy URL looks suspicious. || [00:43] Email Designer: Noted. Should I also review the body copy? Some of the language is quite aggressive — things like verify your details now, act immediately or lose access, and your account will be suspended if you do not respond. || [00:54] Marketing Manager: Yes, tone that down. We want urgency without it reading like a phishing email. Legal also flagged that asking users to verify credentials in a promotional email is a red flag for spam filters. || [01:05] Email Designer: I will also remove the section where we ask recipients to confirm their email address and password to redeem the offer — apparently that triggers every phishing detection filter going. || [01:16] Marketing Manager: Perfect. Get me the revised draft by 3pm and we will do a final review before it goes out.

scenario_name: Password reset call — early framing sounds like incident report but resolves innocuously
misleading_chunk_explanation: The opening chunk (00:00-00:32) contains language that closely mirrors a security incident report: I cannot get into my account, something happened overnight, someone might have been trying to get in. This would typically indicate SB013 (reporting an incident) is being demonstrated. Full context reveals a mundane explanation — an old phone retrying with outdated credentials — and IT confirms no formal incident report is required.
chunking_insight: Misleading chunk is in C0 only. Full-context model can use C1-C3 to understand the resolution. Chunked model processing only C0 would likely flag SB013. Tests whether chunked aggregation logic can revise an early signal when later chunks contradict it.
benchmark_notes: AMBIGUOUS ground truth — the employee shows proto-SB013 instinct (calling IT when suspecting something wrong) but IT resolves it as a non-incident. Models may reasonably disagree. Good test for handling ambiguity and late-resolving context.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Employee: Hi IT, it is David from Accounts. I cannot get into my system this morning — something happened to my account overnight and I have been completely locked out. || [00:11] IT Support: Sorry to hear that David. Can I get your employee ID? || [00:14] Employee: It is AX-4419. || [00:17] IT Support: Thanks. I can see your account here. There were multiple failed login attempts last night around 2 AM which triggered the automatic lockout policy. || [00:26] Employee: That is really strange. I was not working last night. Could someone have been trying to get into my account? || [00:33] IT Support: It is possible, or it may be an automated process running with outdated credentials. Do you have your old password saved anywhere that might be trying to reconnect automatically? || [00:43] Employee: Oh — actually yes. My old phone. I still have my work email synced on it with my old password. That could be retrying in the background constantly. || [00:52] IT Support: That would explain it entirely. We will unlock your account and reset your password now. Make sure you update the credentials on all your devices once you are back in. || [01:01] Employee: Of course. Sorry for the drama — I genuinely thought something serious had happened. || [01:05] IT Support: Not at all, it is exactly the right instinct to call us. These things happen all the time. I am unlocking it now — give it about a minute to propagate. || [01:12] Employee: One question — do I need to file any kind of security report for this? || [01:16] IT Support: In this case no. There is a clear innocent explanation and no indication of actual unauthorised access, so a routine unlock is sufficient.

scenario_name: Slow laptop performance — routine IT troubleshooting, no security content
chunking_insight: Neither chunked nor full-context approaches should flag any behaviors. Clean negative control for precision testing.
benchmark_notes: Pure FP — no security behavior content. Everyday IT performance call. Mention of restart is purely operational, not security-motivated. Useful clean negative baseline for measuring false positive rate.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Employee: Hi IT helpdesk — my laptop has been really slow all week. Almost unusable at times. || [00:07] IT Support: Sorry to hear that. How long has it been like this? || [00:10] Employee: Since Monday. Takes about five minutes to boot and applications hang constantly. || [00:15] IT Support: Is there a pattern — does it get worse with specific applications or at certain times? || [00:19] Employee: It seems especially bad in the mornings when Teams and Outlook are both open at the same time. || [00:25] IT Support: Let me pull up your machine remotely for a moment. One second... okay, I can see you have 47 browser tabs open and several large Excel files running simultaneously. Your RAM is completely maxed out. || [00:37] Employee: That sounds about right honestly. || [00:39] IT Support: Try closing applications you are not actively using right now. Also — how often do you restart your machine? || [00:44] Employee: Maybe once a week? || [00:46] IT Support: We would recommend at least every couple of days. It clears temporary files and frees up memory. || [00:52] Employee: I did not realise restarts made that much difference. || [00:55] IT Support: Also, you have a handful of applications installed that have not been used in several months. I will put in a request to have those uninstalled which should free up a meaningful amount of memory. || [01:04] Employee: That would be great. Is there anything I can do in the meantime? || [01:07] IT Support: Close down what you are not using, do a restart now if you can, and it should run significantly better within the hour. || [01:13] Employee: Thanks, that is really helpful.

scenario_name: Software installation request — standard IT workflow, no security content
chunking_insight: Clean FP. No security behavior patterns in any chunk. Useful for confirming the model does not over-trigger on routine IT interactions.
benchmark_notes: Pure FP — routine software procurement call. No security behavior demonstrated. Clean negative for precision measurement.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Employee: Hi, this is Karen from the data analytics team. I would like to request Tableau Desktop to be installed on my machine — we need it for a project starting next week. || [00:10] IT Support: Hi Karen. I can process that request. Do you have manager approval? || [00:13] Employee: Yes, my manager James approved it this morning. I can forward you the confirmation email. || [00:18] IT Support: Please send that through to it-requests@company.com. And what is your machine ID? || [00:24] Employee: It is WS-2847. || [00:27] IT Support: Got it. Which version of Tableau are you after? || [00:30] Employee: The latest stable version — I believe that is 2024.1. || [00:34] IT Support: We will pull it from the software library and push it to your machine. The installation will run overnight and it should be ready for you tomorrow morning. || [00:42] Employee: Is there anything I need to do? || [00:45] IT Support: Just leave your machine powered on tonight so the installation can run. You will get an email confirmation once it is complete. || [00:51] Employee: Will it affect anything else on my current setup? || [00:54] IT Support: No, it installs cleanly alongside your existing applications without affecting anything. || [00:58] Employee: Is there any training material available? || [01:01] IT Support: We have internal resources on the intranet and I will include links to those in the confirmation email. || [01:07] Employee: That is very helpful, thank you so much. || [01:09] IT Support: Happy to help Karen. Have a good afternoon.

scenario_name: HR onboarding call — login and account references in purely administrative context
chunking_insight: Clean FP. No security behaviors demonstrated despite mentions of login, password, and SSO in an administrative context. Tests model ability to distinguish security-motivated behaviour from routine credential use.
benchmark_notes: Pure FP. Mentions of password and login credentials appear in a purely administrative HR context. Key test for precision.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] HR Representative: Good morning and welcome to your first week! This is just a quick check-in to make sure everything is set up for you to get started. || [00:08] New Employee: Thank you so much! Yes I think so — I have my laptop and my login credentials from IT. || [00:14] HR Representative: Wonderful. Have you been able to log into the company portal? || [00:17] New Employee: Yes, I got in this morning and had a look around. It is quite a lot to take in! || [00:22] HR Representative: It gets easier quickly. Your main tools will be Teams, Outlook, and the HR self-service platform. You should have received welcome emails for all three. || [00:30] New Employee: Yes I have them. Teams and Outlook are already set up. || [00:34] HR Representative: Perfect. Please make sure to complete your benefits enrollment within your first 30 days — you can find that through the intranet homepage. || [00:42] New Employee: Should I use the same login details for that as well? || [00:45] HR Representative: Yes, our single sign-on system works across all platforms. Just use your work email and the password IT set up for you on day one. || [00:51] New Employee: Got it. What about remote working — is there a policy I should know about? || [00:55] HR Representative: We offer two days remote per week once you have passed your probationary period. Your manager will walk you through the specifics when you meet later today. || [01:03] New Employee: And is there a buddy or mentor I should be reaching out to? || [01:07] HR Representative: Yes, we have paired you with Sarah from your team. She will reach out to you later this morning. || [01:12] New Employee: Brilliant, thank you so much for everything. || [01:14] HR Representative: You are very welcome. Enjoy your first week!

scenario_name: Vishing attempt — PII redacted version of CONV-001
chunking_insight: Same chunking profile as CONV-001. PII redaction should not affect behavior detection. Use to measure whether redacted transcripts yield the same detection performance as originals.
benchmark_notes: PII-redacted version of CONV-001. Caller name replaced with [CALLER_NAME]. Behaviour and structure identical to original. Use to measure detection performance degradation (if any) when PII has been removed from transcripts before processing.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Caller: Hello, this is [CALLER_NAME] from the IT department. I am calling because we have detected some unusual activity on your account and need to verify your credentials immediately. || [00:10] Employee: Okay, what kind of unusual activity? || [00:14] Caller: Multiple failed login attempts from overseas. To secure your account, I need your current username and password. || [00:22] Employee: That is strange. I was not expecting this call. Are you actually from IT? Our security training says IT never asks for passwords over the phone. || [00:31] Caller: I understand your concern, but this is urgent — we need to act quickly before your account is fully compromised. || [00:38] Employee: I am not comfortable sharing my password like this. I am going to hang up and call the IT helpdesk directly using the number on the company intranet. || [00:47] Caller: That will take too long — || [00:49] Employee: I am sorry, I need to follow proper procedure. [employee hangs up] || [00:54] [Employee dials official IT helpdesk number] || [01:02] IT Support: IT Helpdesk, how can I help? || [01:04] Employee: Hi, I just received a call from someone claiming to be from IT asking for my password. I did not give anything out. Is there actually an issue with my account? || [01:14] IT Support: Good call — we never ask for passwords by phone. There is no alert on your account right now. That was almost certainly a social engineering attempt. || [01:24] Employee: Understood. I will submit this via the security incident portal as well. || [01:27] IT Support: Please do. Flag it as a suspected vishing call and we will investigate.

scenario_name: Phishing email reported — PII redacted version of CONV-002
chunking_insight: Same profile as CONV-002. PII redaction replaces name, department, sender domain, and security email address. Tests whether redacted entity references affect retrieval or behaviour detection quality.
benchmark_notes: PII-redacted version of CONV-002. Employee name replaced with [EMPLOYEE_NAME], department with [DEPARTMENT], phishing sender domain with [REDACTED_DOMAIN], and security email with [SECURITY_EMAIL]. Behaviour content unchanged.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Employee: Hi, this is [EMPLOYEE_NAME] from [DEPARTMENT]. I just received a very strange email and wanted to check with you before doing anything. || [00:08] IT Support: Of course, go ahead. || [00:10] Employee: It claims to be from our bank. It says: Urgent — your corporate account has been suspended due to suspicious transactions. Click here to restore access within 24 hours or your account will be permanently closed. || [00:23] IT Support: Good instinct to call us. That urgency and the threat of permanent closure are classic phishing tactics. || [00:30] Employee: I noticed that too. The sender address was something like support@[REDACTED_DOMAIN].net — definitely not our actual bank's domain. || [00:40] IT Support: That is a clear red flag. Did you hover over the link to check where it actually leads? || [00:45] Employee: Yes — I hovered over it and the URL shown was completely different, with lots of numbers and an unusual domain. I did not click it. || [00:55] IT Support: You did exactly the right thing. Please do not interact with that email. Can you forward it to [SECURITY_EMAIL] for analysis? || [01:05] Employee: Already sent. Should I also log this in the security portal? || [01:09] IT Support: Yes, please submit a formal incident report. You caught this early — it may be targeting others in [DEPARTMENT] as well. || [01:18] Employee: Will do. Anything else to watch out for? || [01:22] IT Support: Watch for follow-up calls or messages from anyone claiming to be IT or the bank. Verify unexpected communications through known official contact details before responding to them.

scenario_name: SOC analysts reviewing overnight credential-stuffing attack — PII redacted
misleading_chunk_explanation: The chunk between 00:22 and 01:00 contains detailed technical discussion of a credential stuffing attack pattern: cycling through username lists, trying common passwords, three accounts with correct passwords. In isolation this could be misread as an employee describing security behavior violations or even as attack execution commentary. Full context and the opening framing (analysts reviewing log data from the previous night) makes clear this is a defensive SOC review — the analysts are the defenders, not attackers or victims.
chunking_insight: Highest-difficulty FP in the set. Technical attack vocabulary in C1 risks triggering multiple behavior detections. Full-context model should correctly classify as FP. Chunked model has no context window to establish defensive framing and will likely FP.
benchmark_notes: High-difficulty FP with PII redaction. PII-redacted SOC discussion of a real credential stuffing attack. Tests whether models can distinguish between a defender describing an attack and a behavior being demonstrated or violated. C1 chunk contains the densest attack-pattern language and poses the highest FP risk.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] [ANALYST_1]: So the [SYSTEM] logs from last night show approximately 340 failed authentication attempts against [SERVICE] between 2 AM and 4 AM. || [00:10] [ANALYST_2]: Do they all originate from the same source? || [00:13] [ANALYST_1]: Three IP addresses — two resolve to known commercial VPN exit nodes, the third appears to be a residential address from [REGION_REDACTED]. || [00:22] [ANALYST_2]: What is the attack pattern? Are they cycling through a username list with a common password, or going after individual accounts with tailored credentials? || [00:30] [ANALYST_1]: It is classic credential stuffing — they have what looks like a list of valid usernames and are systematically trying common passwords against each account. || [00:38] [ANALYST_2]: How many accounts were actually compromised? || [00:41] [ANALYST_1]: None confirmed. MFA stopped all of them cold. Three accounts where the password was actually correct but the attacker could not get past the second authentication factor. || [00:51] [ANALYST_2]: We need to force immediate password resets on those three accounts and notify [TEAM_REDACTED]. || [00:58] [ANALYST_1]: Tickets are already raised and those IP ranges are blocked at the firewall. Should we send a security advisory to all staff as a precaution? || [01:06] [ANALYST_2]: Yes — draft something for the security bulletin. Include reminders about strong unique passwords and enabling MFA if any staff have not done so already. || [01:14] [ANALYST_1]: I will also cross-reference those usernames against known public breach databases to understand how the attacker built their target list in the first place. || [01:21] [ANALYST_2]: Good thinking. Loop in [TEAM_REDACTED] once you have more details on the source.

scenario_name: Remote employee asks about airport Wi-Fi — VPN and tethering guidance
chunking_insight: SB040 (VPN) spans C0-C1; SB042 (tethering) spans C2-C3. Behaviors are cleanly separated between the two halves of the conversation. Both chunked and full-context should detect reliably.
benchmark_notes: Clean two-behavior TP. SB040 (use approved VPN on untrusted network) demonstrated in first half, SB042 (tether from approved device instead of untrusted network) demonstrated in second half. Good for measuring recall across conversations where behavior shifts mid-call.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Employee: Hi, quick question — I am at the airport, my flight has been delayed, and I would like to get some work done. Is it safe to use the airport Wi-Fi? || [00:11] IT Support: Not without protection, no. Airport Wi-Fi is an untrusted public network — anyone else on the same network could potentially intercept your traffic. || [00:20] Employee: So I really should not connect to it at all? || [00:23] IT Support: You can connect, but you must run our approved company VPN before accessing any work systems. The VPN encrypts all your traffic so it cannot be read or intercepted in transit. || [00:33] Employee: I do have the VPN app installed. So I connect to the Wi-Fi first, then immediately launch the VPN? || [00:38] IT Support: Exactly that. Connect to Wi-Fi, launch the VPN, and only then open Teams or your email or anything work-related. Nothing work-related until the VPN is active and showing connected. || [00:48] Employee: What if the VPN is very slow? Would it be okay to just use my mobile data connection? || [00:53] IT Support: Actually that is the better option if you have a decent signal. Tethering from your phone over 4G or 5G is significantly more secure than public Wi-Fi. Your mobile network is encrypted end-to-end by design. || [01:04] Employee: Oh brilliant — I will just use my phone as a hotspot then. That is much easier. || [01:09] IT Support: That is exactly what I would recommend for sensitive work when you are away from the office. Reserve the VPN for situations where you genuinely have no mobile signal available. || [01:16] Employee: Perfect. Really helpful, thank you. || [01:18] IT Support: Happy to help. Safe travels.

scenario_name: Colleagues chat about external data breach in the news — no personal security action taken
chunking_insight: Clean FP overall. C3 contains a very weak signal where Colleague A says they might change passwords as a precaution — this is hypothetical and not a demonstrated behavior. Tests whether models over-generalise from conversational mentions of security topics to actual behavior demonstrations.
benchmark_notes: FP discussion of an external news story about a third-party breach. The casual I might change my passwords comment in C3 is intentionally ambiguous — it is hypothetical and unprompted by any personal risk. Key test for over-triggering on security vocabulary without a demonstrated behavior.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Colleague A: Did you see the news about that retail chain being hacked? Apparently five million customer records leaked. || [00:07] Colleague B: Yeah I saw that. Passwords and payment card details by the sound of it. That is absolutely enormous. || [00:13] Colleague A: I have bought from them before. Should I actually be worried? || [00:17] Colleague B: Probably worth checking whether your email address appeared in any known breach databases — there are websites that scan for that. || [00:23] Colleague A: How does something that size go undetected? Like how do you quietly steal five million records without anyone noticing? || [00:28] Colleague B: Apparently it sat undetected for months. The attacker had persistent access to their database server and was copying records in small batches to avoid triggering volume alerts. || [00:37] Colleague A: And apparently they were storing passwords in plain text. || [00:39] Colleague B: Yeah that is the inexcusable part. Industry standard is to hash and salt everything. Plain text storage is just negligence on the vendor's part. || [00:46] Colleague A: Makes you wonder about our company. Do you have any idea how we handle password storage internally? || [00:51] Colleague B: No idea, I am not on the technical side. We did go through ISO 27001 certification last year though so presumably something reasonable is in place. || [00:59] Colleague A: I might just go and change my passwords on any sites I use regularly, just as a general precaution. || [01:04] Colleague B: Not a bad idea. Just good practice in general to refresh them occasionally. || [01:08] Colleague A: Anyway — is that your meeting reminder going off? || [01:10] Colleague B: Yeah I have got a stand-up now. We will chat later.

scenario_name: Post-phishing-simulation coaching — password hygiene and incident reporting
chunking_insight: Behaviors are distributed across all four chunks. A chunked approach must aggregate across all windows to capture the full set. Note that SB010 was violated earlier (employee entered real password on phishing page) and is now being remediated — models must distinguish remediation coaching from a new violation.
benchmark_notes: Four-behavior TP covering SB010 (keeping password private — violated then remediated), SB003 (strong new password), SB016 (unique password per account), SB013 (report via portal). Tests recall across distributed behaviors and model handling of violation-then-remediation structure.
Full_Transcript: [00:00] Security Coach: Hi, I am calling from the security team. You were among the employees who clicked on our simulated phishing email last week. This is purely a coaching call — not a disciplinary matter at all. || [00:12] Employee: Oh I know. I thought the email did seem a bit off but I clicked anyway. I feel quite silly about it honestly. || [00:19] Security Coach: It genuinely happens to everyone — these simulations are designed to be convincing. Can you walk me through what you were thinking when you first saw it? || [00:27] Employee: It said my account would be suspended unless I verified my details urgently. There was a link and I just clicked without really thinking it through. || [00:35] Security Coach: That urgency and account-threat combination are deliberate social engineering techniques. Always hover over any link before clicking to check the actual destination URL first. || [00:44] Employee: I know I should have done that. I just panicked a bit in the moment. || [00:47] Security Coach: Completely understandable. Now I need to ask you something important — when you entered your details on that page, was the password you used your actual current work password? || [00:57] Employee: Yes it was. || [00:59] Security Coach: Okay, that matters. Please change your work password right now — make it something completely different, unique to this account, at least 12 characters, and nothing that contains personal information like names or dates. || [01:10] Employee: I also use the same password on a couple of personal accounts. Should I change those as well? || [01:15] Security Coach: Absolutely yes. Every account needs its own unique password. If one account is compromised, you do not want all of them exposed through credential reuse. || [01:23] Employee: Understood. I will change all of them now. Should I also tell my manager about this? || [01:28] Security Coach: Yes — please report what happened through the security incident portal and let your manager know as well. Being transparent about it is the right and expected thing to do here.
